+ make run-san debe felicitarlo y no reportar ningún incidente en el

+ make run debe felicitarlo por aprobar este modo de ejecución.

+ make run-g debe felicitarlo.

Cuando pruebe su tarea con make run en su computador asegúrese de que

Opcionalmente puede probar su tarea con make run-thr, pero tenga en cuenta que

tarea.  Haga esta prueba si su tarea no está funcionando para determinar

archivo postulacion.zip que contiene postulacion.c, con su solución, y resultados.txt,
con la salida de make run, make run-g y make run-san.

Para depurar use: make ddd

Video con ejemplos de uso de ddd: https://youtu.be/FtHZy7UkTT4

Ejecute make zip si necesita generar resultados.txt junto con la solución.

Acerca del comando make

El comando make sirve para automatizar el proceso de compilación asegurando
recompilar el archivo binario ejecutable cuando cambió uno de los archivos
fuentes de los cuales depende.

A veces es útil usar make con la opción -n para que solo muestre
exactamente qué comandos va a ejecutar, sin ejecutarlos de verdad.
Por ejemplo:

   make -n ddd

También es útil usar make con la opción -B para forzar la recompilación
de los fuentes a pesar de que no han cambiado desde la última compilación.
Por ejemplo:

# Para revisar las opciones de compilacion y ejecucion,
# ingrese en el terminal el comando: make
#
#

PROB=postulacion

ARCH=$(shell arch)

SRCS= test-$(PROB).c $(PROB).c pss.c spinlocks.c swap-$(ARCH).s
HDRS= $(PROB).h pss.h spinlocks.h

TEST=test-$(PROB)
HEADER=$(PROB)

SHELL=bash -o pipefail

INCLUDE=
#CFLAGS=-mcpu=cortex-a76 -Wall -Werror -std=c18 $(INCLUDE)
CFLAGS=-Wall -Werror -pedantic -std=c18 $(INCLUDE)
LDLIBS=-lm -lpthread

MAK=make --no-print-directory

readme:
	@less README.txt

$(PROB).bin $(PROB).bin-g $(PROB).bin-san $(PROB).bin-thr: $(SRCS) $(HDRS)

run-san: $(PROB).bin-san
	@if grep -P '\t' $(PROB).c ; then echo "Su archivo $(PROB).c contiene tabs.  Reemplacelos por espacios en blanco!" ; false ; else true; fi
	./$(PROB).bin-san

run-thr: $(PROB).bin-thr
	./$(PROB).bin-thr sin busy waiting

run-g: $(PROB).bin-g
	./$(PROB).bin-g

run: $(PROB).bin
	./$(PROB).bin

ddd: $(PROB).ddd

ddd-san: $(PROB).ddd-san

zip:
	@if grep -P '\t' $(PROB).c ; then echo "Su archivo $(PROB).c contiene tabs.  Reemplacelos por espacios en blanco!" ; false ; else true; fi
	@rm -f resultados.txt $(PROB).zip
	@echo "Sistema operativo utilizado" > resultados.txt
	@uname -a >> resultados.txt
	@cat resultados.txt
	@echo ==== run-san ==== | tee -a resultados.txt
	@$(MAK) -B run-san | tee -a resultados.txt
# No se corre make run-thr porque toma demasiado tiempo
#	@echo ==== run-thr ==== | tee -a resultados.txt
#	@$(MAK) -B run-thr | tee -a resultados.txt
	@echo ==== run-g ==== | tee -a resultados.txt
	@$(MAK) -B run-g | tee -a resultados.txt
	@echo ==== run ==== | tee -a resultados.txt
	@$(MAK) -B run | tee -a resultados.txt
	@echo ==== zip ====
	zip -r $(PROB).zip resultados.txt $(PROB).c
	@echo "Archivo $(PROB).zip generado"
	@echo "Revise que contiene su ultima version de $(PROB).c"

%.bin: %.c
	gcc -O -DOPT=1 $(CFLAGS) $(SRCS) $(LDLIBS) -o $@

%.bin-g: %.c
	gcc -g $(CFLAGS) $(SRCS) $(LDLIBS) -o $@

%.bin-san: %.c
	gcc -g -DSAN=1 -fsanitize=address -fsanitize=undefined $(CFLAGS) $(SRCS) $(LDLIBS) -o $@

%.bin-thr: %.c
	gcc -g -DSAN=1 -DSANTHR -fsanitize=thread $(CFLAGS) $(SRCS) $(LDLIBS) -o $@

%.ddd: %.bin-g
	ddd $(*F).bin-g &

%.ddd-san: %.bin-san
	ddd $(*F).bin-san &

clean:
	rm -rf *.o *.log *.bin* core bin obj resultados.txt $(PROB).zip

content.text:
Let me understand the task:

1. The task is a concurrent programming problem in C about job applications ("postulaciones").
2. There's a system with N jobs and N students. Students apply to jobs, and jobs are closed one by one.
3. The original code has data races and needs to be fixed using spin-locks (provided in spinlocks.h/.c).
4. We must implement `iniciarPostulaciones()`, `destruirPostulaciones()`, `postularTrabajo()`, and `cerrarPostulacion()` in `postulacion.c`.
5. We cannot use mutex/cond/semaphores - must use spin-locks.
6. The original implementation has busy-waiting loops without synchronization.

Let me understand the algorithm:

**Original implementation (in the task description):**

```c
void postularTrabajo(Estudiante *est, int *preferencias, double *rank) {
  est->trabajo_id = -1;
  Postulacion postulacion = {est, rank};
  for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) {
    if (postulacionTrabajos[i]==-1 && preferencias[i])
      priPut(priQueue[i], &postulacion, rank[i]);
  }
  while (est->trabajo_id == -1)
    ;
}

int cerrarPostulacion(int i) {
  while (postulacionTrabajos[i]==-1) {
    while(!emptyPriQueue(priQueue[i])) {
      Postulacion *ppostulacion = priGet(priQueue[i]);
      if (ppostulacion->est->trabajo_id == -1) {
        postulacionTrabajos[i] = ppostulacion->est->id;
        ppostulacion->est->trabajo_id = i;
        for(int j = 0; j < N; j++)
          priDel(priQueue[j], ppostulacion);
        break;
      }
    }
  }
  return postulacionTrabajos[i];
}
```

**What the problem says:**
- Several students apply to at most N jobs (only N available).
- Students can only get one job.
- A student can only apply if a job posting is "open".
- `priQueue` is an array of priority queues for each job.
- `postulacionTrabajos[i]` = id of the student who got job i, or -1 if not assigned.
- `postularTrabajo` is called by each student (one thread per student).
- `cerrarPostulacion(int i)` is called by each job thread to close job i.

**Data race issues:**
1. Shared variables `postulacionTrabajos[i]`, `priQueue[i]`, `est->trabajo_id` are accessed without synchronization.
2. Multiple students access `postulacionTrabajos[i]` concurrently.
3. `postularTrabajo` and `cerrarPostulacion` access `priQueue[i]` concurrently.
4. `est->trabajo_id` is read/written by both student and job threads.

**What I need to add:**
- Spin-locks to protect shared data.
- The `Postulacion` struct can have additional parameters.
- I can create more global variables.

Let me think about the synchronization design:

1. Each job i needs a spin-lock to protect access to `priQueue[i]` and `postulacionTrabajos[i]`.
2. We also need a way for students to wait until they get a job (busy-waiting is allowed per the original code).
3. We need to handle the case where `cerrarPostulacion` runs before any student has applied.

Actually, looking more carefully at the original:

In `postularTrabajo`:
- The student loops through all N jobs. For each job that is still open (postulacionTrabajos[i]==-1) and preferred, they add their application to the priority queue.
- Then they busy-wait until `est->trabajo_id != -1`.

In `cerrarPostulacion(int i)`:
- Busy-wait until `postulacionTrabajos[i] != -1`.
- While the queue is not empty, get the highest-priority applicant.
- If that applicant hasn't been assigned a job yet (`trabajo_id == -1`), assign them to this job.
- Remove this applicant from all other queues.
- If the applicant already has a job, skip them and try the next.
- After the loop, if no applicant was found, the inner while loop ends but the outer while continues (busy-wait for more applicants).

Wait, actually the original has a potential issue: if `cerrarPostulacion` runs and the queue is empty, the inner while loop doesn't execute, and the outer while continues (forever if no more students will arrive). But in the tests, they create student threads first, wait, then create job threads, so jobs see students in the queue.

However, test3 creates job threads first, then waits, then creates student threads. In this case, `cerrarPostulacion` will spin until students put items in the queue. So the busy-waiting on empty queue is needed.

Actually wait, let me re-read the original more carefully:

```c
int cerrarPostulacion(int i) {
  while (postulacionTrabajos[i]==-1) {
    while(!emptyPriQueue(priQueue[i]) {
      Postulacion *ppostulacion = priGet(priQueue[i]);
      if (ppostulacion->est->trabajo_id == -1) {
        postulacionTrabajos[i] = ppostulacion->est->id;
        ppostulacion->est->trabajo_id = i;
        for(int j = 0; j < N; j++)
          priDel(priQueue[j], ppostulacion);
        break; // SE AGREGÓ ESTE BREAK !
      }
    }
  }
  return postulacionTrabajos[i];
}
```

When the queue is empty, `!emptyPriQueue(priQueue[i])` is false, so we skip the inner loop and go back to the outer while loop. This means we spin-wait checking if the queue becomes non-empty AND postulacionTrabajos[i] is still -1. That's fine for correctness but very inefficient.

But actually, once `postulacionTrabajos[i]` is set, the outer while loop exits, so the function returns.

Now let me think about the synchronization:

For each job i, I need:
- A spin-lock `lock[i]` to protect `priQueue[i]` and `postulacionTrabajos[i]`.

Also, we need a way for students to wait until their `trabajo_id` is set. The original uses busy-waiting which is fine (allowed by the task).

Actually, the task says "no se preocupe de la hambruna" (don't worry about starvation), so busy-waiting is acceptable.

Let me design the solution:

Global variables:
```c
spinlock_t locks[N];  // One lock per job
```

In `iniciarPostulaciones()`:
- Initialize each spin-lock
- Initialize priQueue and postulacionTrabajos

In `destruirPostulaciones()`:
- Destroy each priQueue

In `postularTrabajo()`:
- For each job i:
  - Lock job i
  - If postulacionTrabajos[i] == -1 and preferencias[i]:
    - priPut(priQueue[i], &postulacion, rank[i])
  - Unlock job i
- Busy-wait until est->trabajo_id != -1

In `cerrarPostulacion(int i)`:
- While postulacionTrabajos[i] == -1:
  - Lock job i
  - While !emptyPriQueue(priQueue[i]): // this is the critical section
    - ppostulacion = priGet(priQueue[i])
    - if ppostulacion->est->trabajo_id == -1:
      - postulacionTrabajos[i] = ppostulacion->est->id
      - ppostulacion->est->trabajo_id = i
      - for j in 0..N-1:
        - Lock job j
        - priDel(priQueue[j], ppostulacion)
        - Unlock job j
      - Unlock job i
      - break (exit inner loop, which also exits outer because postulacionTrabajos[i] != -1)
    - else: continue (try next in queue)
  - Unlock job i
  - (If we exit the inner while but postulacionTrabajos[i] is still -1, we go back to outer while loop)

Wait, but there's a subtle issue. When we lock inside cerrarPostulacion, we do `priGet` which modifies the priority queue. We need the lock to be held during this operation. Also, when we remove entries from other queues, we need to lock those queues too.

But there's a classic locking order issue: we can get deadlocks if two cerrarPostulacion threads try to lock each other's jobs while one holds its own lock.

To avoid deadlock, let's use a lock ordering: always lock lower-index jobs first. Or we could use a simpler approach.

Actually, looking at the original code more carefully:

In `cerrarPostulacion(i)`:
1. It gets the top applicant from queue[i].
2. If that applicant hasn't been assigned yet, it assigns job i to them.
3. Then it removes them from ALL other queues (priDel on all queues).

So `cerrarPostulacion` needs to access all queues. If we lock each queue individually when accessing, we need to be careful about lock ordering.

But actually, let me think about what `priDel` does - it searches the queue's array for the element and removes it. This involves reading/writing the internal array of the queue.

One approach: Have a single global lock. But this would be a bottleneck. However for N=100 it should be fine.

Actually, to match the original semantics and keep it simple, let's use one lock per job, and when needed, lock all of them in a fixed order (to avoid deadlock).

But wait, there's another approach. Let me re-examine the problem:

The task description says we need to implement the functions using spin-locks. The test file uses pthreads but our implementation should use the provided spin-locks from spinlocks.h.

Let me look at the spinlock interface:
```c
enum { OPEN, CLOSED};
int setBusyWaiting(int flag);
void spinLock(volatile int *psl);
void spinUnlock(int *psl);
```

So a spinlock is just a volatile int, initially OPEN.

Let me think about a cleaner design:

For each job i, we have a spinlock `lock[i]` that protects:
- `priQueue[i]`
- `postulacionTrabajos[i]`

Now, in `postularTrabajo`:
- For each job i: lock[i] must be held while reading `postulacionTrabajos[i]` and `priPut` on `priQueue[i]`.

In `cerrarPostulacion(i)`:
- Lock[i] must be held while reading/writing `priQueue[i]` and `postulacionTrabajos[i]`.
- When removing the applicant from other queues, we need to lock those queues too.

To avoid deadlock when locking multiple queues:
- Always lock in increasing order of job index.

So in `cerrarPostulacion(i)`:
1. Lock[i] (because it's our own queue)
2. While queue[i] not empty:
   - Get top applicant
   - If applicant is free:
     - Assign job
     - For j in 0..N-1, if j != i:
       - Lock[j] (in increasing order)
       - priDel(priQueue[j], ppostulacion)
       - Unlock[j]
     - Unlock[i]
     - Return
3. Unlock[i]
4. (If we reached here, queue is empty but job not assigned - spin again)

Wait, but if we hold lock[i] while trying to lock other queues, and another thread holds lock[j] while trying to lock queue[i], we'd have deadlock. But since we always lock in increasing order (first lock[i], then lock[i+1], etc.), and the other thread also locks in increasing order, there's no circular wait.

Actually, let me reconsider. If cerrarPostulacion(i) holds lock[i] and tries to lock lock[j] where j > i, and cerrarPostulacion(j) holds lock[j] and tries to lock lock[k] where k > j, that's fine because j > i so they don't conflict.

But what if cerrarPostulacion(0) holds lock[0] and tries lock[1], while cerrarPostulacion(1) holds lock[1] and tries lock[2]? No problem because 1 doesn't try to lock 0. 

What about cerrarPostulacion(0) holds lock[0], tries lock[1]. cerrarPostulacion(1) holds lock[1], tries lock[0]? This would be deadlock! But since we always lock in increasing order, cerrarPostulacion(1) wouldn't try to lock 0 because it's lower. So no deadlock.

But what if cerrarPostulacion(i) holds lock[i] and then tries lock[j] where j < i? Let me think... Actually, in the code:
- cerrarPostulacion(i) first locks lock[i] (its own queue).
- Then, when removing from other queues, it iterates j from 0 to N-1 and locks lock[j].

If i=5, it holds lock[5], then tries lock[0], lock[1], ..., lock[4], lock[6], ...
If another thread in cerrarPostulacion(3) holds lock[3], then tries lock[0], lock[1], lock[2], lock[4], ...
- Thread 1 (i=5): holds lock[5], wants lock[3]
- Thread 2 (i=3): holds lock[3], wants lock[4]... but doesn't want lock[5]
So no deadlock because:
- Thread 1 holds 5, wants 0,1,2,3,4,6,...
- Thread 2 holds 3, wants 0,1,2,4,5,6,...
They both want lock[4] eventually, but since they acquire in increasing order, whomever gets lock[4] first will proceed.

Actually wait, thread 2 (i=3) holds lock[3], then would try to acquire lock[0], lock[1], lock[2] (which are lower, already unlocked), lock[4]... But hold on, if we're doing the "remove from all queues" operation, we first lock our own queue (lock[i]), then iterate j from 0..N-1, locking lock[j] each time.

Thread 1 (i=5): holds lock[5]. Iterates j=0: lock[0] (acquired), unlock[0]. j=1: lock[1] (acquired), unlock[1]. ... j=3: lock[3] (tries to acquire).
Thread 2 (i=3): holds lock[3]. This is where we get stuck because thread 1 is trying to acquire lock[3] which thread 2 holds.

But thread 2 (i=3) also needs to remove from other queues. It holds lock[3] and then iterates j=0: lock[0], unlock[0]. j=1: lock[1], unlock[1]. j=2: lock[2], unlock[2]. j=4: lock[4], unlock[4]. j=5: lock[5], tries to acquire...

So thread 1 holds lock[5] and wants lock[3].
Thread 2 holds lock[3] and wants lock[5].
Deadlock!

To avoid this, we should NOT hold lock[i] while locking other queues. We should:
1. Lock lock[i], get the applicant, unlock lock[i].
2. Then lock all queues we need to modify (in fixed order) to do the removal.

But we have a TOCTOU issue: between step 1 and 2, another thread could modify the queues. However, if the applicant has already been assigned to job i (trabajo_id set to i), and we're just removing from other queues, it's not critical because the removal is just for cleanup. The key invariant is that an applicant is only assigned once (trabajo_id goes from -1 to a non-negative value once).

Actually, looking at the original algorithm more carefully:

The original `cerrarPostulacion(i)`:
1. Gets the top applicant from queue[i].
2. If the applicant hasn't been assigned yet (trabajo_id == -1):
   - Assigns the applicant to job i.
   - Removes the applicant from ALL other queues.
3. If the applicant was already assigned (trabajo_id != -1), it was removed from queue[i] and we try the next one.

This means that when we remove the applicant from other queues, we're doing cleanup to prevent that applicant from being selected for other jobs. It's not strictly necessary for correctness (the check `if (ppostulacion->est->trabajo_id == -1)` in other cerrarPostulacion threads would prevent double-assignment), but it's an optimization to avoid iterating through stale entries.

So for the removal step, we could potentially skip locking other queues if we accept that some stale entries might remain (they'll be skipped by the check). But the task says to maintain the original functionality, so we should implement the removal.

Let me redesign to avoid the deadlock:

**Approach: Use a single global lock**

Actually, the simplest approach is to use one global spin-lock that protects all shared state. For N=100 with mostly simple operations, contention should be low enough.

But wait, let me think about what operations need to be atomic:

In `postularTrabajo`:
- Reading `postulacionTrabajos[i]` and adding to `priQueue[i]` should be atomic per job i.

In `cerrarPostulacion(i)`:
- Reading/writing `priQueue[i]` and `postulacionTrabajos[i]` should be atomic.
- Removing from other queues should also be atomic w.r.t. other threads.

Actually, a per-queue approach with careful lock ordering could work. Let me try:

In `cerrarPostulacion(i)`:
1. Lock lock[i] (our queue lock).
2. While queue[i] not empty:
   - Get applicant from queue[i].
   - If applicant is free (trabajo_id == -1):
     - Assign: postulacionTrabajos[i] = est->id, est->trabajo_id = i.
     - Unlock lock[i].
     - For each j != i:
       - Lock lock[j].
       - priDel(priQueue[j], ppostulacion).
       - Unlock lock[j].
     - Return.
   - Else:
     - Continue (try next in queue).
3. Unlock lock[i].
4. (Queue empty, go back to outer while loop - spin wait)

This avoids deadlock because we never hold two locks at the same time. But we have a TOCTOU issue: after we unlock lock[i] and before we lock other queues, another cerrarPostulacion thread could modify those queues. But that's fine because priDel just removes the entry if it exists; if it was already removed, it's a no-op (well, actually priDel would return an error code but we don't care).

Wait, what about the case where between unlocking lock[i] and locking lock[j], another cerrarPostulacion thread assigns the same student to job j? Let's check:

The student's `trabajo_id` is already set to `i` (not -1), so the other cerrarPostulacion would see `trabajo_id != -1` and skip this student. So the assignment is safe. The removal is just cleanup.

Actually, there's still a potential issue. In the original:

```c
if (ppostulacion->est->trabajo_id == -1) {
    postulacionTrabajos[i] = ppostulacion->est->id;
    ppostulacion->est->trabajo_id = i;
    for(int j = 0; j < N; j++)
        priDel(priQueue[j], ppostulacion);
    break;
}
```

The check `trabajo_id == -1` and the assignment are meant to be atomic. If we don't keep the lock during this, another thread could set `trabajo_id` between the check and our assignment. But actually, since we got the applicant from queue[i] (protected by lock[i]), and the check passed, we then set `trabajo_id = i` and `postulacionTrabajos[i] = id`. The key question is: can another thread set `trabajo_id` between the check and our set?

```c
static volatile int global_lock = OPEN;

void iniciarPostulaciones() {
    for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) {
        priQueue[i] = makePriQueue();
        postulacionTrabajos[i] = -1;
    }
    global_lock = OPEN;
}

void destruirPostulaciones() {
    for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) {
        destroyPriQueue(priQueue[i]);
    }
}

void postularTrabajo(Estudiante *est, int *preferencias, double *rank) {
    est->trabajo_id = -1;
    Postulacion postulacion = {est, rank};
    for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) {
        if (preferencias[i]) {
            spinLock(&global_lock);
            if (postulacionTrabajos[i] == -1) {
                priPut(priQueue[i], &postulacion, rank[i]);
            }
            spinUnlock(&global_lock);
        }
    }
    while (*(volatile int*)&est->trabajo_id == -1)
        ;
}

int cerrarPostulacion(int i) {
    while (1) {
        spinLock(&global_lock);
        if (postulacionTrabajos[i] != -1) {
            spinUnlock(&global_lock);
            break;
        }
        while (!emptyPriQueue(priQueue[i])) {
            Postulacion *ppost = (Postulacion*)priGet(priQueue[i]);
            if (ppost->est->trabajo_id == -1) {
                // Assign
                postulacionTrabajos[i] = ppost->est->id;
                ppost->est->trabajo_id = i;
                
                // Drain remaining entries in this queue
                while (!emptyPriQueue(priQueue[i])) {
                    priGet(priQueue[i]);
                }
                
                // Remove from all other queues
                for (int j = 0; j < N; j++) {
                    if (j != i) {
                        priDel(priQueue[j], ppost);
                    }
                }
                
                spinUnlock(&global_lock);
                return postulacionTrabajos[i];
            }
        }
        spinUnlock(&global_lock);
    }
    return postulacionTrabajos[i];
}
```

To make it atomic, we could use a global student lock or add a lock to each student/Postulacion.

Actually, let me think one more time. The task says "manteniendo la funcionalidad de estas". The original `postularTrabajo` and `cerrarPostulacion` had the race condition where two cerrarPostulacion threads could assign the same student. In practice, the race is unlikely because:
1. After cerrarPostulacion(i) assigns a student, it removes the student from all other queues.
2. cerrarPostulacion(j) won't see this student in its queue anymore (since it was removed).

But what if cerrarPostulacion(j) had already retrieved the student from its queue before the removal? In the original code without synchronization, this could happen.

With per-job locks:
1. cerrarPostulacion(i) locks job_locks[i], gets student X from queue[i], checks trabajo_id == -1 (true), and is about to assign.
2. cerrarPostulacion(j) locks job_locks[j], gets student X from queue[j], checks trabajo_id == -1 (true), and is about to assign.

But with per-job locks AND the removal:
1. cerrarPostulacion(i) locks job_locks[i], gets student X, checks trabajo_id == -1, assigns.
2. cerrarPostulacion(i) then iterates j, locks job_locks[j], removes X from queue[j].
3. cerrarPostulacion(j) locks job_locks[j]... but step 2 hasn't happened yet. cerrarPostulacion(j) might have already locked job_locks[j] before step 2.

## Solución completada

### Archivo modificado: `/workspace/postulacion.c`

### Resumen de la solución

Se implementaron las cuatro funciones (`iniciarPostulaciones`, `destruirPostulaciones`, `postularTrabajo`, `cerrarPostulacion`) usando un **spin-lock global** (`global_lock`) para proteger todas las variables compartidas:

- `priQueue[i]` – cola de prioridad del trabajo `i`
- `postulacionTrabajos[i]` – arreglo con el id del estudiante asignado al trabajo `i` (-1 si libre)
- `est->trabajo_id` – campo que indica qué trabajo obtuvo cada estudiante (-1 si ninguno)

### Decisiones de diseño

1. **Un solo spin-lock global**: Simplifica la implementación y elimina cualquier posibilidad de deadlock. Dado que N=100 y las operaciones críticas son cortas (inserción/extracción en un heap binario), la contención es baja y el rendimiento es adecuado para los tests.

2. **Doble verificación (`double-check`)**: En `postularTrabajo`, primero se verifica `preferencias[i]` sin el lock, luego se adquiere el lock y se vuelve a verificar `postulacionTrabajos[i] == -1` antes de insertar. Esto asegura atomicidad entre la lectura y la inserción.

3. **Drenado de la cola**: En `cerrarPostulacion`, después de asignar un estudiante a un trabajo, se drena el resto de la cola del trabajo (los `PriObj` restantes se liberan con `priGet`) para evitar fugas de memoria.

4. **Acceso volátil**: El bucle de espera activa (`while (est->trabajo_id == -1)`) usa un acceso `volatile` para evitar que el compilador lo optimice como bucle infinito.

5. **Sin cambios en los archivos de cabecera**: No se modificaron `postulacion.h`, `pss.h`, `spinlocks.h` ni ningún otro archivo.

Los 5 tests se ejecutan correctamente tanto con la implementación de spinlocks basada en mutex/condición como con la implementación de busy-waiting real (instrucciones atómicas en ensamblador).
